items.title: Lifecycle Marketing 101
items.summary: Scaling your marketing outreach as a small business? Then you may be familiar with the concept of lifecycle marketing. Here's the non-technical definition: it is a form of marketing that targets your audience based on their behavior across the entire customer journey. It's a roadmap…
items.content_html:
Scaling your marketing outreach as a small business? Then you may be familiar with the concept of lifecycle marketing. Here's the non-technical definition: it is a form of marketing that targets your audience based on their behavior across the entire customer journey.

What is a customer journey? 

It's a roadmap that starts with awareness, leads to purchases, and hopefully cultivates long-term value for your business.

A successful lifecycle marketing approach involves building brand trust and crafting content that resonates with customers across each stage of their relationship with your business.

Ready to get started with lifecycle marketing? Read on for your quick-start guide.

Build your ideal audience

If you think your audience is "everyone" — think again. Creating a well-defined audience will help you maximize your marketing dollars.

First, build 3-5 brand personas — detailing the characteristics of your ideal buyer. Selling athletic socks? Perhaps your target personas can include mountain sports enthusiasts in their 30's-40's Selling SaaS? Your personas may include chief marketing officers in mid-sized corporations.

Avoid making too many personas and stick with several that encompass the type of customer who can be best served by your brand. Each persona should identify:

  • Motivators: business needs and concerns
  • Factors that will make or break the sale
  • Decision-making process and purchase style

Once you have personas in place, it's time to map out their behavior across the customer lifecycle. How long should a customer lifecycle be It varies by industry. 

Typically these stages are:

  • Awareness — educating and community-building
  • Consideration —  decision making
  • Purchase — closing the sale
  • Post-Purchase — nurturing long-term relationships

Get a feel for how long your customer lifecycle should be by analyzing competitors. Sign up for their marketing lists and scope out how they are timing and positioning specific promotions. You can also consult market intelligence sources — from analyst reports to business media. 

A deeper dive into lifecycle stages

Awareness

The awareness stage is your opportunity to edge out competitors. Think beyond social media — there are other ways to build brand awareness. B2B clients can emphasize online listings, accolades, and awards. Small businesses and major retailers may benefit from optimizing online reviews and local PR outreach.

Consideration

This is the make-or-break stage where customers follow through on a purchase or abandon their cart. Be sure to answer their frequently asked questions about your products and services — while reassuring them that they are making the best decision for their needs. That extra nudge may mean showcasing a customer testimonial or case study — or detailing your guarantees and policies. 

Purchase

Don't underestimate the power of thank-you messaging. If you're looking to build a brand in a competitive market, this is your chance to reinforce your brand. To encourage future conversions, make it easy for customers to share their experience with friends and family. And make it easy to customize how they'd like to interact with your brand moving forward. Always provide an incentive for joining a list and make it easy to update their marketing preferences.

Post-Purchase

Unfortunately, brands are likely to hear from unhappy shoppers. Make a change by adding seamless ways to review and share your brand across the customer lifecycle. Surveys and share buttons are popular ways to gather insights while growing follower count and engagement. 

When potential customers see their community partaking in your brand, it plants a seed in their minds when the time comes to make a purchase. You can amplify this effect by implementing a solid customer loyalty program.

Choose the right tools

Once you've established brand personas and mapped out your customer lifecycle stages, it's time to choose the right mix of marketing tools. Today's customer is largely mobile — they have a lot of choices, are highly informed, and seeking brands that align with their personal identities.

What types of marketing tools should you invest in initially? At a minimum, be sure to choose a tool for:

  • Customer relationship management
  • Email
  • Social media

For e-commerce businesses, an SMS (text messaging) platform is highly recommended. For B2B businesses, a solid content management platform is ideal.

Sweeten the sale with content

You've all heard content is king/queen. But exactly how do you personalize content for the customer lifecycle? 

Start with a good content calendar. It will help your team — brand, PR and comms, email, creative, legal, and leadership — all get on the same page. Today's digital buyers are seeking a diverse mix of marketing content that answers their questions and delivers on their needs.

The ideal content calendar covers all the types of content you want to deploy — from blogs to social media. Create a cadence that naturally flows with consumer demand. If your peak seasons are fall and winter, it makes sense to deploy more overt calls to action during that period.

Make sure to leave room in your content calendar for creative outreach and one-off campaigns. As consumer needs and preferences change, you'll need to have the ability to deploy custom messaging to ensure that they receive the right information at the right time.

Ready to evolve your lifecycle marketing?

As you improve your lifecycle marketing outreach, it's time to make the most of the rich data you've gathered. Don't let this information sit out for too long or save it for end-of-the-year business planning. A well-organized dashboard is essential to helping your team make informed decisions. 

As you iterate and optimize your process, you'll ideally create an effective process that adapts to your target customer's evolving needs, while achieving your business goals.

items.title: Email marketing basics for first timers
items.summary: Most business owners understand the power of email marketing. It's a great way to grow sales and build community. But are you ready to step your email game up? First — check on your content. Is it fresh and regularly updated? Second, do you have…
items.content_html:
Most business owners understand the power of email marketing. It's a great way to grow sales and build community. 

But are you ready to step your email game up? 

First — check on your content. Is it fresh and regularly updated? Second, do you have compelling visuals and a clear brand that will resonate with buyers? If you've answered yes, you are ready to get started with email marketing. Here's a step-by-step guide.

Start with your competitors

Looking at where you stand in the market is key to making the right marketing investments. Generate a list of your top 10 competitors and sign up for their email flows. Analyze their copy, creative, brand positioning, timing, and offers to get a send of how you can compete.

How to choose the right email tool

Choosing the right email tool can be overwhelming. Here are some examples: 

  • Braze
  • Constant Contact
  • Klaviyo
  • Litmus
  • Mailchimp
  • Sailthru
  • SendLoop
  • SendPulse
  • Segment

How to create an email list

Rule number one — keep a clean list. Then make sure to understand your customer. You can segment an email list by level of intent, demographics, location, and behavior. It's also important to ensure that users have opted in. Explain exactly what you will do and what they can expect (how often you'll message, types of content. 

Depending on your email service provider, you can implement automatic list clean-up and organization. For example, you can add users who've clicked on your shopping page link within the last 30 days to "active users" — while purging users who haven't interacted with your brand in the last year. Dynamically updating your list helps you pinpoint users with the most likelihood of opening your messages.

Finally, be sure you are upholding your branding and advertising policies. Keep up to date with legal guidelines such as the CAN-SPAM Act and GDPR, for example 

Metrics and KPIs

You've likely heard that email contributes to increasing customer lifetime value (LTV) — driving long-term engagement, repeat sales, and referrals. But here are some other metrics to track and what to aim for. 

  • Clicks and CTR (2-5%)
  • Open rate (15-25%)
  • Spam rate (less than 1%)
  • Unsubscribes (less than 1%)

What are the basic email flows?

Welcome Flow

First impressions are everything. Your welcome email is a way to showcase your value proposition and educate the customer on why they made the right decision. 

The goal is to educate — and show social proof that they've joined a great community. Try linking to case studies and highlighting customer testimonials — or touting recent accolades and high ratings.

You'll also want to draw attention to core features and benefits. And make sure to clearly communicate important policies, such as guarantees, warranties, shipping, and returns.  

Post-Purchase Flow

Customers have short attention spans, so thank them ASAP. Generally, you should be following up within one hour. 

The thank-you email is a great (and may be your only) opportunity for retargeting. Popular features for post-purchase messaging include:

  • Add-on discounts and bundle
  • Rewards for sharing with friends and family
  • Opt-ins to ongoing offers

You should also cement a relationship with customers by immediately asking for an opt-in to future marketing messages. Don't make the mistake of signing them up automatically — they may opt out and you'll lose a customer for life!

Abandoned Cart Flow

For customers taking their time on a purchase, it's up to sweeten the offer.

For example, create a catchy headline that emphasizes the product's price and benefits. You can also highlight convenience — such as a 100% guarantee or 24/7 support. 

How to write a good email

Think like a human, think like a buyer, and be a good writer.

Hiring a professional copywriter may also be a worthwhile investment. Poor copy and clickbait will get flagged by email providers — and it will annoy potential customers. 

Good email copy has a clear goal and right-size elements based on consumer behavior — not assumptions. Lead with user needs and nurture your community.

To get feedback on your email content, be sure to follow the rule of three. One person from branding, one from legal, and one decision maker (who says yes/no to the copy) is the most you need.

How to design a good email

Choosing high-quality images and videos is critical. Online buyers are spoiled for choice these days. 

Keep your design high-contrast and easy to read, avoiding more than 3 calls-to-action in total. That way, the customer will know exactly where to click to get what they need, fast.

How to create good email offers

There are three segments of consumers who benefit from email offers: new users, loyal customers, and those at risk of leaving. Here are some good ideas for offers:

New users
Codes for sharing with friends and family
One-time discounts

Loyal customers
Add-ons and bulk discounts
Rewards program offers
Seasonal deals

At-risk of leaving
Timed discounts
Free trials and samples

How to test emails

At a minimum, emails should be tested on all browsers and major email providers — plus mobile devices. Then, you may want to consider testing how specific elements perform.

A/B testing or split testing an email campaign starts by establishing a baseline for statistical significance (P value). Each test should go out to 1,000 users at minimum for the results to be statistically significant.

It's unwise to rely on your email service provider to automatically deploy the "winning" email. Popular elements to test in emails include copy, design, offers, and behaviors.

What's next in email marketing?

Once you've gotten into a good flow with emails, be sure to continuously test and seek out opportunities for improvement. As the market changes, so will the makeup of your customer base. 

You may find that what works one year needs tweaking the next.

items.title: Case Study: Kickstarting A Boutique Gym’s Brand Visibility
items.summary: Problem A boutique personal training studio had the desire to market its premier circuit training studio in the heart of Kensington but lacked the bandwidth to manage its social media presence. Potential clients in the East Bay generally lacked knowledge of the boutique women-owned gym,…
items.content_html:
Problem

A boutique personal training studio had the desire to market its premier circuit training studio in the heart of Kensington but lacked the bandwidth to manage its social media presence. Potential clients in the East Bay generally lacked knowledge of the boutique women-owned gym, opting for national chains with higher brand visibility.

Solution

Marina Bay Media worked with the studio to engage high-potential clients via social media and online. We built a modern, responsive website optimized with content to attract upwardly mobile fitness enthusiasts in the East Bay Area. We also nurtured and grew the gym’s caring, supportive community with regular online posts, email newsletters, and online reviews management. 

Follow-Up

Inbound leads and referrals via social media continue to pour in for the fitness studio. It has recently expanded to a new location in Albany, and has added more personal trainers to its staff to expand its offerings.

Contact Marina Bay Media Today

items.title: Case Study: Alameda Wine Importer Achieves International Acclaim
items.summary: Problem Two first-time entrepreneurs launched a wine distribution startup and sought assistance with an integrated communications plan to gain visibility in the crowded California wine market. The team lacked assets for their first round of digital and in-person outreach — as well as training on…
items.content_html:
Problem

Two first-time entrepreneurs launched a wine distribution startup and sought assistance with an integrated communications plan to gain visibility in the crowded California wine market. The team lacked assets for their first round of digital and in-person outreach — as well as training on the best mix of tools to achieve their goals. They had been experimenting with basic social media outreach but had not experienced any tangible success.

Solution

Marina Bay Media worked together with the two business owners to ramp up its local brand presence via web and social media. First, a modern, responsive website was built on the WordPress platform — showcasing product features and tasting notes from its first shipment of premium wines from a lesser-known European region. 

As small producers themselves, the company was interested in communicating in a fresh, approachable way to its consumers — seasoned wine drinkers and those experiencing wine for the first time. Marina Bay Media collaborated with certified winemaking experts to craft web copy that resonated with a diverse online audience. 

Follow-Up

Within the first month of public relations outreach, Marina Bay Media secured media coverage on a high-profile food and wine publication — hailing the company’s mission of sourcing rare wines from a unique and untapped region. Other media opportunities secured included travel magazines and in-person sampling events.

The company’s social media engagement also improved — with increased engagement and sales activity — direct to consumer and wholesale. Today, the company boasts over a dozen featured wineries and can be found in prominent Bay Area restaurants and retailers.

Contact Marina Bay Media Today

items.content_html:

We also implemented conversion tracking and a real-time marketing performance dashboard to help with daily decision-making and presentations at monthly marketing meetings.

The company’s social media engagement also improved — with more comments and shares, likes, and follows.

items.title: Case Study: Data Breach Response and WordPress Website Development for Bay Area Solar Company
items.summary: Problem This year, the CEO of a Bay Area solar company contacted us for assistance in addressing a data breach in their website and workspace accounts from several former contractors and business partners. The individuals, based in several countries, managed to hack into systems containing…
items.content_html:
Problem

This year, the CEO of a Bay Area solar company contacted us for assistance in addressing a data breach in their website and workspace accounts from several former contractors and business partners. The individuals, based in several countries, managed to hack into systems containing sensitive proprietary data and personal information, resulting in business disruption. In addition to online business assets, the hackers had also managed to access the business owner's personal email account.

Solution

Marina Bay Media worked quickly to ensure the physical and online security of the solar company's assets — starting with a detailed response plan of action to ensure future security. First, we implemented a secure password-sharing tool — right-sizing access levels to ensure future compliance. Second, we offered recommendations on contacting local authorities and law enforcement about the breach. Finally, we offered ongoing assistance to regain control of critical business accounts. 

Follow-Up

In addition to the initial data breach response, Marina Bay Media also restored the company's website. First, the site was scanned for and cleansed of malware and bloated content. We also implemented a lean, faster theme to improve user experience on the web and mobile devices — and updated content to streamline the online shopping experience.

Contact Marina Bay Media Today

If you are a business owner interested in creating a robust online and social media presence — and protecting your business reputation, contact Marina Bay Media. items.title: Google Ad Must-Haves for First Timers
items.summary: Creating your first Google Ads campaign? Read on for tips on getting started the right way. With Google algorithms constantly changing, creating goals and metrics can be intimidating. Here's what to focus on first: Lifetime Value (LTV)= (average order size) * (purchase frequency) * (lifespan…
items.content_html:
Creating your first Google Ads campaign? Read on for tips on getting started the right way.

Have goals and set the right metrics

With Google algorithms constantly changing, creating goals and metrics can be intimidating. Here's what to focus on first:

Lifetime Value (LTV)= (average order size) * (purchase frequency) * (lifespan — how long a customer stays a customer)

After calculating LTV, it's time to figure out your customer acquisition cost (CAC). CAC is the measurement of what you should be spending to get customers in the door. 

Having a low LTV to CAC ratio — in the ballpark of 3:1 is generally thought of as good. Obviously, you want to spend as little as possible on getting new customers. 

However, there may be reasons to aim for a higher CAC. For example, perhaps you're selling high-ticket items and want to focus on "quality over quantity." Or you may have a smaller budget and are willing to spend more for targeted leads.

Once you get a sense of the amount you'd like to invest, it's time to set up your KPIs, conversion tracking, and metrics. Google Analytics has great built-in functionality, but you may want to use other tools.

Look beyond your audience

Understanding your audience is important — but defining it is only the first step. Great ad copy draws the reader in by targeting their desires, pain points, and other motivating factors. 

Put yourself in the shoes of your target clients and customers. Better yet, build tools to ask them what they want, what they like, and don't like. 

If you'd like to use data to inform your decisions, use cohort analysis. Cohort analysis is essentially noting data on how users behave as a distinct group. A cohort could be periodic — customers who purchase a product on a certain season — or it can be behavioral — customers who clicked on a social media ad and clicked on your phone number.

Client feedback programs, polls, and forms are also good ways to gather key learnings on what audiences may prefer to see in an ad.

Let the writers write

Ad copywriting without clear expectations is a doomed endeavor. The seasoned ad copywriter often encounters roadblocks in the form of unnecessary bureaucracy. 

Do 15 people really need to approve a 30-character headline? Probably not. A comprehensive copywriting and style guide is a great way to get writers, editors, and other stakeholders on the same page. 

Leverage tech with a human touch

Generative AI and similar platforms may sound like a great tool for creating ad content, but there is a risk of (1) plagiarizing content unintentionally and (2) being unoriginal.

Your audience is human — so take the time to create ads that speak to their core needs. Today's most successful ads have several elements: 

  • Attention-grabbing headlines and descriptions
  • Relevant search terms and keywords
  • Unique value proposition and selling points

Don't neglect the landing page

A common frustration online users face is getting to a page that does not match the ad content. You only have a few seconds to capture their attention, so be sure to incorporate plenty of calls to action and the exact information they are looking for. 

Use tools such as Looker Studio to monitor the success of your campaigns — sharing key learnings with your team on at least a weekly basis to improve performance. A/B testing is a great way to maintain consistency while optimizing for the best-fit mix of assets, headlines, descriptions, and calls to action.

Remember compliance

Google Ads policies are constantly changing so be sure to double-check whether you may be advertising for a potentially controversial topic. Common problem areas include personalized health, copyrights and trademarks, gambling, alcohol, and adult content.

What now?

As you embark on your Google Ads journey, you may notice that there is a lot of change occurring at the moment. Primarily, there has been a major move to Google Analytics 4 (GA4). This is an updated method of tracking user engagement across your web properties that's quickly changing the state of SEM advertising. 

How will Google Analytics 4 affect Google Ads? The first is measurement. New metrics like engaged sessions, engagement rate, and engagement time will allow you to hone in on where users are in the customer journey — and develop tactics at scale to rein them in. GA4 also features a predictive audiences feature that will allow you to target likely purchasers or engage your target users at scale.

If you'd like any assistance with getting started, please reach out to our online ad experts today.

items.title: Technical Writing for Non-Technical Folks
items.summary: Consider yourself a steward of language, yet a layperson in technical subjects? Fear not — a guide to technical writing for non-technical writers is below. We hope this approach will help you confidently cross over that writer's slump! Generic questions beget generic answers. Approach your…
items.content_html:
Consider yourself a steward of language, yet a layperson in technical subjects? 

Fear not — a guide to technical writing for non-technical writers is below. We hope this approach will help you confidently cross over that writer's slump!

Think Like a Reporter

Generic questions beget generic answers. Approach your subject matter expert well-equipped with a deep understanding of their product or service offering. Map out questions that are both specific to your needs and broad enough to help them share necessary context.

Ideally, you'll have looked through: 

  • Case studies and white papers
  • Published research
  • Recent press 
  • Thought leadership

A word of caution — do not fall for the trap of replication. For many industries the prevailing content is dry and dull. While your source is an expert in their field, it's up to you to weave what they share into a cohesive narrative.

Diving into a subject for the first time? 

You'll know when it's time to set pen to paper if: 

  • You can explain the product and service to a child — easily able to explain core components and processes
  • You can adjust the copy for different needs — from salespersons to executives
  • You can readily cite the primary thought leaders and top competitors in the industry

A final piece of advice is to understand that technical writing is an ongoing process. Be sure to keep up to date with your company's latest releases by conducting periodic check-ins, monitoring their newsletter, and, most importantly, keeping tabs on their competitors and industry trends.

Happy writing! For more advice on your next campaign, please don't hesitate to reach out directly.
